mission.title: Install and Configure Linode Kubernetes Engine (LKE)
mission.description: Linode Kubernetes Engine (LKE) is a managed Kubernetes service that simplifies the deployment and management of Kubernetes clusters on Linode's infrastructure. It is ideal for users looking for a hassle-free way to run Kubernetes without managing the underlying hardware. Key trade-offs include reliance on Linode's infrastructure and potential costs associated with resource usage.

steps.title: Install the Linode CLI
steps.description:
This step installs the Linode CLI, which is necessary for managing your Linode resources from the command line.

```bash

curl -sL https://github.com/linode/linode-cli/releases/download/v2.0.0/linode-cli-2.0.0-linux-amd64.tar.gz | tar xz -C /usr/local/bin

chmod +x /usr/local/bin/linode-cli

```

> This installs the Linode CLI binary.
> Make the CLI executable.

steps.title: Create a Kubernetes Cluster
steps.description:
This command creates a new Kubernetes cluster on Linode with the specified version and node count.

```bash

linode-cli k8s cluster-create my-cluster --region us-east --k8s-version 1.30 --node-count 3

```

> Replace 'my-cluster' with your desired cluster name.

steps.title: Set Up Kubeconfig
steps.description:
This step configures your kubeconfig file to connect to the newly created cluster.

```bash

linode-cli k8s kubeconfig-view my-cluster --format json > ~/.kube/config

```

> This saves the kubeconfig to your local machine.

steps.title: Verify Cluster Nodes
steps.description:
This command checks the status of the nodes in your Kubernetes cluster to ensure they are running.

```bash

kubectl get nodes

```

> You should see your nodes listed with a 'Ready' status.

steps.title: Post-Install Configuration
steps.description:
This step sets up essential features like autoscaling and monitoring for your cluster.

```bash

kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/linode/linode-cloud-controller-manager/v0.9.5/deploy/ccm-linode-template.yaml

```

> This applies the necessary configurations for the Linode Cloud Controller Manager.

uninstall.title: Delete the Kubernetes Cluster
uninstall.description:
This command removes the Kubernetes cluster and all associated resources. Be cautious as this will result in data loss.

```bash

linode-cli k8s cluster-delete my-cluster

```

> Replace 'my-cluster' with your cluster name.

uninstall.title: Remove the Linode CLI
uninstall.description:
This step removes the Linode CLI from your system.

```bash

rm /usr/local/bin/linode-cli

```

> This deletes the Linode CLI binary.

upgrade.title: Upgrade the Kubernetes Cluster
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you back up your data and configurations. This step upgrades the cluster to a newer version.

```bash

linode-cli k8s cluster-update my-cluster --k8s-version 1.30

kubectl get nodes

```

> Replace 'my-cluster' with your cluster name.
> Verify that the nodes are in 'Ready' state after the upgrade.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Unable to connect to the server: x509: certificate signed by unknown authority'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the kubeconfig file is not set up correctly or the cluster is not reachable.

**Fix:**
Regenerate the kubeconfig using 'linode-cli k8s kubeconfig-view my-cluster --format json > ~/.kube/config'.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'No resources found in default namespace'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that there are no resources deployed in the default namespace.

**Fix:**
Deploy resources using 'kubectl apply -f <your-resource-file.yaml>'.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to create service: service is invalid'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This can occur if the service configuration is incorrect.

**Fix:**
Check the service YAML for errors and correct them.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Insufficient CPU resources available'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the cluster does not have enough resources to schedule new pods.

**Fix:**
Scale up your cluster by adding more nodes with 'linode-cli k8s cluster-update my-cluster --node-count <new-count>'.

versionNotes.changes: Introduced support for new scheduling features and improved resource management.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated certain legacy APIs related to node management.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from 1.29, ensure to review the new scheduling configurations.

resolution.summary: You should see output from 'kubectl get nodes' showing all nodes in 'Ready' status, confirming that the cluster is operational.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have the Linode CLI installed and configured with your API token.
